entry.title: [Optimization] Subgradient Methods
entry.content:
Motivation

How should we handle nondifferentiable objective functions?

  • objective functions can be convex but not differentiable
  • can’t use the standard optimality conditions
  • can’t use gradient or Hessian information in optimization algorithms

Subgradients

Definition

The subgradient set or subdifferential of a function $f$ at a point $x$ is defined as

\[\partial f(x) := \{ g \ \vert \ f(y) \geq f(x) + \langle g, y-x \rangle, \ \ \forall y\}\]

Intuitively, since a function is convex if and only if its epigraph $\text{epi } f$ is convex, the subgradient set $\partial f$ should be non-empty and consist of supporting hyperplanes of $\text{epi }f$.

If $f$ is conevx and diffeerentiable at $x$, then $\partial f(x) = { \nabla f(x)}$.

Optimality Condition

Theorem Let $f: \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be convex. The point $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$ minimizes $f$ over a convex set $C$ if and only if there exists a subgradient $g\in \partial f(x)$ such that simultaneously for all $y\in C$,

\[\langle g, y-x \rangle \geq 0\]

Intuition There is a vector $g$ in the subgradient set $\partial f(x)$ such that $-g$ is a supporting hyperplane to the feasible set $C$ at the point $x$. The direction of decrease of the function $f$ lie outside the set $C$.

If $C =\mathbb{R}^n$, $g=0$ is the only case where above inequality holds.

Calculus rules with subgradients

  • Scaling : if $h(x) = \alpha f(x)$ for some $\alpha \geq 0$, then

    \[\partial h(x) = \alpha \partial f(x)\]

  • Finite sum : Suppose $f_1, \cdots, f_m$ are convex functions and let $f:=\sum_{i=1}^m f_i$. Then,

    \[\partial f(x) = \sum_{i=1}^m \partial f_i (x)\]

    where the addition is Minkowski sum.

    Proof Let $g_i \in \partial f_i(x)$ for all $i$. Then,

    \[\begin{aligned} f(y) = \sum_{i=1}^m f_i (y) & \geq \sum_{i=1}^m (f_i(x)+ \langle g_i , y-x\rangle )\\ & =\sum_{i=1}^m f_i (x) + \bigg \langle \sum_{i=1}^m g_i , y-x \bigg \rangle \end{aligned}\]

    we can see that

    \[\sum_i g_i \in \partial f(x)\]

  • Affine transformations : Let $f: \mathbb{R}^m \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be convex and $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m\times n}$ and $b\in \mathbb{R}^m$. Then, $h: \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ defined by $h(x) = f(Ax+b)$ is convex and has subdifferential

    \[\partial h(x) = A^T \partial f(Ax+b)\]

    Proof Let $g \in \partial f(Ax+b)$. Then,

    \[\begin{aligned} h(y) = f(Ay+b) & \geq f(Ax+b) + \langle g, (Ay+b) - (Ax+b) \rangle\\ & = h(x) + \langle g, A(y-x) \rangle\\ & = h(x) + \langle A^T g, y-x \rangle\\ \end{aligned}\]

    which shows that

    \[A^T g = A^T \partial f(Ax+b)\in \partial h(x)\]

Integrals with subgradients

Suppose that $f_s$ is convex for each $s\in S$, and let

\[f(x):= \int_S f_s (x) d\mu (s).\]

where $\mu$ is a positive measure on $S$. Then, if we let $g_s(x) \in \partial f_s(x)$ for each $s\in S$, we have

\[\int_S g_s (x) d\mu(s) \in \partial f(x)\]

Proof

\[\begin{aligned} \bigg \langle \int_S g_s (x) d\mu(s), y-x \bigg \rangle & = \int \langle g_s(x), y-x \rangle d\mu(s)\\ & \leq \int \Big ( f_s (y) - f_s(x) \Big )d\mu(s)\\ & = f(y)-f(x) \end{aligned}\]

Application to stochastic optimization

If we have a collection of functions $F:\mathbb{R}^n \times \mathcal{S} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$, where for each $s\in S$ the function $F(\cdot; s)$ is convex, then

\[f(x) := \mathbb{E}_s [ F(x; \mathcal{S}) ]\]

is convex when taking expectations over $\mathcal{S}$, and letting

\[g(x; s) \in \partial F(x;s)\]

gives a stochastic subgradient with the property that

\[\mathbb{E}_s [g(x;S)] \in \partial f(x).\]

Finite Maxima

Let $f_i$, $i=1,\cdots, m$ be convex functions and $f(x) := \max_i f_i (x)$. Then,

\[\text{epi }f = \bigcap_i \text{epi }f\]

Then, the subgradient set of $f$ is the convex hull of the subgradients of **active functions ** at $x$ (those attaining the maximum:

\[\partial f(x) = \text{Conv} \{ \partial f_i (x) : f_i(x) = f(x)\}\]

Proof For $y\in \mathbb{R}^n$, let $i$ be any index that $f_i (x) = f(x)$ (active index) and let $g_i \in \partial f_i (x)$.

\[\begin{aligned} f(y) \geq f_i (y) & \geq f_i (x) + \langle g_i, y-x \rangle\\ & = f(x) + \langle g_i, y-x \rangle \end{aligned}\]

Subgradient Methods

Motivation

Subgradient-based methods are essentially universally applicable for convex optimization problems, becuase they rely very little on the structure of the problem being solved.

  • This leads to effective but slow algorithms in classical optimization problems.
  • In large scale problems arising out of ML and statistical tasks, however, subgradient methods enjoy a number of (thoretical) optimality properties and have excellent practical performance.

Subgradient Algorithms

Recall that in gradient descent method, we have

\[x_{k+1} = x_k - \alpha_k \nabla f(x_k)\]

where $\alpha_ k>0$ is a positive sequence of stepsizes.

Then, the subgradient algorithm iterates for $k=1,2,\cdots$, the following step:

  1. choose any subgradient

    \[g_k \in \partial f(x_k)\]

  2. Take the subgradient step

    \[x_{k+1}:= x_k - \alpha_k g_k.\]

Note that, subgradient descent does not guarantee $f(x_{k+1}) \not \leq f(x_k)$.

In this case, however, subgradients at least descend on a related quantity: the distance of $x$ to any optimal point.

Proof Let $g\in \partial f(x)$ and let $x^\star \in \arg\min f(x)$. Then, for any $\alpha$,

\[\frac{1}{2} \|x - \alpha g-x^\star\|_2^2 = \frac{1}{2}\|x - x^\star\|_2 ^2 -\alpha \langle g, x-x^\star\rangle + \frac{\alpha^2}{2} \|g \|_2^2\]

Then, by the definition of subgradients,

\[-\langle g, x-x^\star \rangle = \langle g, x^\star - x\rangle \leq f(x^\star) - f(x)\]

Therefore,

\[\frac{1}{2}\|x- \alpha g -x^\star\|_2^2 \leq \frac{1}{2} \|x- x^\star\|_2^2 -\alpha (f(x) - f(x^\star) ) + \frac{\alpha^2}{2}\|g\|_2 ^2.\]

We then conclude that, if $0<\alpha< \frac{2(f(x) - f(x^\star))}{|g|_2^2}$,

\[\|x- \alpha g- x^\star \|_2 ^2 \leq \|x - x^\star \|_2 ^2\]

If $0\not\in \partial f(x)$, then for any $x^\star\in \arg\min_x f(x)$ and any $g\in \partial f(x)$, there exists a stepsize $\alpha>0$ such that \(\|x-\alpha g -x^\star\|_2^2 < \|x-x^\star\|_2^2\).

Convergence Gaurantees

We begin by stating a general result on the convergence of subgradient methods. We make a few simplifying assumptions in stating our result, several of which are not completely necessary, but which considerably simplify the analysis.

Assumption

  1. There is at least one (possibly non-unique) minimizing point \(x^\star \in \arg\min_x f(x)\) with \(f(x^\star) = \inf_x f(x) >-\infty\).
  2. The subgradients are bounded: for all $x$ and all $g\in \partial f(x)$, we have the subgradient \(\|g\|_2 \leq M <\infty\) (independently of $x$).

Theorem Let $\alpha_k$ be any non-negative sequence of stepsizes and the preceding assumptions hold. Let $x_k$ be generated by the subgradient iteration. Then for all $K\geq 1$,

\[\sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k \Big [ f(x_k) - f(x^\star) \Big ] \leq \frac{1}{2} \|x_1 - x^\star\|_2^2 + \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k^2 M^2\]

Proof By using equation (20),

\[\begin{aligned} \frac{1}{2}\|x_{k+1}- x^\star\|_2^2 & \leq \frac{1}{2}\|x_k - \alpha_k g_k - x^\star\|_2^2\\ & \leq \frac{1}{2} \|x- x^\star\|_2^2 - \alpha_k (f(x_k) - f(x^\star) ) + \frac{\alpha^2}{2}\|g_k\|_2^2 \end{aligned}\]

Rearranging this inequality, and using the bounded assumption \(\|g_k\|_2^2 \leq M^2\), we obtain

\[\alpha_k (f(x_k) -f(x^\star))\leq \frac{1}{2} \|x_k - x^\star\|_2^2 - \frac{1}{2}\|x_{k+1}- x^\star\|_2^2 + \frac{\alpha_k^2}{2}M^2\]

By summing above equation from $k=1$ to $k=K$, we obtain

\[\sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k \Big [ f(x_k) - f(x^\star) \Big ] \leq \frac{1}{2} \|x_1 - x^\star\|_2^2 + \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k^2 M^2\]

Weighted Averaging

Corollary Let $A_k:= \sum_{i=1}^k \alpha_i$ and define

\[\bar{x}_k = \frac{1}{A_k} \sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k x_k.\]

Then, we have

\[f(\bar{x}_k) -f(x^\star) \leq \frac{\|x_1 -x^\star\|_2^2 + \sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k^2 M^2}{2\sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k}\]

Proof Note that $A_k^{-1} \sum_i \alpha_i =1$, and convexity of $f$ implies

\[\begin{aligned} f(\bar{x}_k) - f(x^\star) &\leq \frac{1}{\sum_i \alpha_i} \sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k f(x_k) - f(x^\star)\\ & = A_k^{-1} \bigg [ \sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k (f(x_k)- f(x^\star)) \bigg ] \\ & =\frac{\|x_1 - x^\star\|_2^2 + \sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k^2 M^2}{2A_k} \end{aligned}\]

Remarks

  • Suppose that

    \[\alpha_k \rightarrow 0 \ \ \ \ \text{and} \ \ \ \ \sum_{k=1}^\infty \alpha_k = \infty\]

    Then,

    \[\frac{\sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k^2}{\sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k} \rightarrow 0\]

    and thus

    \[f(\bar{x}_K) - f(x^\star) \rightarrow 0 \ \ \text{as} \ \ K\rightarrow \infty\]

References

  • S. Boyd, L.Vandenberghe, Convex Opimization, Cambridge University Press, 2004. http://stanford.edu/~boyd/cvxbook/
  • Optimization Theory and Applications, SNU EE 2021, Insoon Yang

ot\in \partial f(x)$, then for any $x^\star\in \arg\min_x f(x)$ and any $g\in \partial f(x)$, there exists a stepsize $\alpha>0$ such that \(\|x-\alpha g -x^\star\|_2^2 < \|x-x^\star\|_2^2\). We make a few simplifying assumptions in stating our result, several of which are not completely necessary, but which considerably simplify the analysis. Assumption There is at least one (possibly non-unique) minimizing point \(x^\star \in \arg\min_x f(x)\) with \(f(x^\star) = \inf_x f(x) >-\infty\). The subgradients are bounded: for all $x$ and all $g\in \partial f(x)$, we have the subgradient \(\|g\|_2 \leq M <\infty\) (independently of $x$). Theorem Let $\alpha_k$ be any non-negative sequence of stepsizes and the preceding assumptions hold. Let $x_k$ be generated by the subgradient iteration. Then for all $K\geq 1$, \[\sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k \Big [ f(x_k) - f(x^\star) \Big ] \leq  rac{1}{2} \|x_1 - x^\star\|_2^2 +  rac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k^2 M^2\] Proof By using equation (20), \[ egin{aligned}  rac{1}{2}\|x_{k+1}- x^\star\|_2^2 & \leq  rac{1}{2}\|x_k - \alpha_k g_k - x^\star\|_2^2\ & \leq  rac{1}{2} \|x- x^\star\|_2^2 - \alpha_k (f(x_k) - f(x^\star) ) +  rac{\alpha^2}{2}\|g_k\|_2^2 \end{aligned}\] Rearranging this inequality, and using the bounded assumption \(\|g_k\|_2^2 \leq M^2\), we obtain \[\alpha_k (f(x_k) -f(x^\star))\leq  rac{1}{2} \|x_k - x^\star\|_2^2 -  rac{1}{2}\|x_{k+1}- x^\star\|_2^2 +  rac{\alpha_k^2}{2}M^2\] By summing above equation from $k=1$ to $k=K$, we obtain \[\sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k \Big [ f(x_k) - f(x^\star) \Big ] \leq  rac{1}{2} \|x_1 - x^\star\|_2^2 +  rac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k^2 M^2\] Weighted Averaging Corollary Let $A_k:= \sum_{i=1}^k \alpha_i$ and define \[ ar{x}_k =  rac{1}{A_k} \sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k x_k.\] Then, we have \[f( ar{x}_k) -f(x^\star) \leq  rac{\|x_1 -x^\star\|_2^2 + \sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k^2 M^2}{2\sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k}\] Proof Note that $A_k^{-1} \sum_i \alpha_i =1$, and convexity of $f$ implies \[ egin{aligned} f( ar{x}_k) - f(x^\star) &\leq  rac{1}{\sum_i \alpha_i} \sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k f(x_k) - f(x^\star)\ & = A_k^{-1}  igg [ \sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k (f(x_k)- f(x^\star))  igg ] \ & = rac{\|x_1 - x^\star\|_2^2 + \sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k^2 M^2}{2A_k} \end{aligned}\] Remarks Suppose that \[\alpha_k ightarrow 0 \ \ \ \ 	ext{and} \ \ \ \ \sum_{k=1}^\infty \alpha_k = \infty\] Then, \[ rac{\sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k^2}{\sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k} ightarrow 0\] and thus \[f( ar{x}_K) - f(x^\star) ightarrow 0 \ \ 	ext{as} \ \ Kightarrow \infty\] References S. Boyd, L.Vandenberghe, Convex Opimization, Cambridge University Press, 2004. http://stanford.edu/~boyd/cvxbook/ Optimization Theory and Applications, SNU EE 2021, Insoon Yang

entry.title: [Optimization] Interior Point Methods
entry.content:
Constrained Minimization Problems

Problem Definition

In this post, we will consider methods for inequality constrained minimization problem:

\[\begin{aligned} \min &\ \ f_0(x)\\ \text{s.t.}& \ \ f_i (x) \leq 0, \ i=1,\cdots, m\\ & \ \ Ax=b \end{aligned}\]

where $f_0, \cdots, f_m : \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ are convex and twice continuously differentiable with $A \in \mathbb{R}^{p\times n}$ with $\text{rank }A = p <n$. We assume that the problem is solvable with optimal $x^\star$ and optimal value $p^\star$. We assume that the problem is strictly feasible, which means that Slater’s constraint holds, thus strong duality holds.

In other words, there exist dual optimal $\lambda^\star \in \mathbb{R}^m$ and $\nu^\star \in \mathbb{R}^p $ which together with $x^\star$ satisfy the KKT conditions.

\[\begin{aligned} Ax^\star & = b\\ f_i (x^\star) & \leq 0 \ \ (i=1,\cdots, m)\\ \lambda^\star & \geq 0\\ \nabla f_0 (x^\star) +\sum_{i=1}^m \lambda^\star_i \nabla f_i (x^\star) + A^T \nu^\star & =0\\ \lambda_i^\star f_i (x^\star)& =0 \ \ (i=1,\cdots, m) \end{aligned}\]

Logarithmic Barrier

Our goal is to approximately formulate the inequality constrained problem as an equality constraint problem to which Newton’s method can be applied. To do so, we can rewrite the problem, making the inequality constraints implicit in the objective:

\[\begin{aligned} \min_x & \ \ f_0 (x) + \sum_{i=1}^m I_- (f_i (x))\\ \text{s.t.}& \ \ Ax =b \end{aligned}\]

where $I_-: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is the indicator function for the nonpositive reals,

\[I_- (u) = \begin{cases} 0 & \ u\leq 0\\ \infty & \ u>0 \end{cases}\]

Now, the problem has no inequality constraints, but its objective function is not differentiable, thus Newton’s method cannot be applied. Thus, we might come up with an idea to use an approximate function of $I_-$, replaced by the function

\[\hat{I}_-(u) = -\frac{1}{t} \log (-u)\]

which is convex and increasing in $u$, and differentiable. Substituting $\hat{I}-$ for $I-$ in above problem, we can get

\[\begin{aligned} \min_x & \ \ f_0 (x) + \sum_{i=1}^m -(1/t) \log(-f_i (x))\\ \text{s.t.}& \ \ Ax =b \end{aligned}\]

If we define

\[\phi(x) = - \sum_{i=1}^m \log (-f_i (x)), \ \ \text{dom } \phi = \{ x \ \vert \ f_i (x) <0, \ i=1, \cdots, m\}\]

we can see that $\phi$ is convex and twice continuously differentiable with derivatives

\[\nabla \phi(x) = \sum_{i=1}^m -\frac{1}{f_i (x)}\nabla f_i (x)\\ \nabla ^2 \phi(x) = \sum_{i=1}^m \bigg [ \frac{1}{f_i(x)^2 }\nabla f_i (x) \nabla f_i (x)^T -\frac{1}{f_i(x)}\nabla ^2 f_i (x) \bigg ]\]

Using $\phi$, we rewrite the problem

\[\begin{aligned} \min_x & \ \ tf_0 (x) + \phi(x)\\ \text{s.t.}& \ \ Ax =b \end{aligned}\]

Central Path

For $t>0$, we define $x^\star(t)$ as the solution of above problem. The central path associated with this problem is defined as the set of point $x^\star(t), t>0$ which we call the central points.

\[\{x^\star(t) \ \vert \ t>0\}\]

Points on the central path are characterized by the following necessary and sufficient conditions:

\[Ax^\star(t) =b\\ f_i (x^\star(t)) <0, \ \ i=1,\cdots, m\\ t\nabla f_0 (x^\star(t)) + \nabla \phi(x^\star(t))+ A^T\hat{v} =0\]

Note that first two conditions imply that slater’s condition, thus strong duality holds. Also, the third condition implies that there exists $\hat{\nu}$ such that

\[0= t\nabla f_0 (x^\star(t) ) + \sum_{i=1}^m -\frac{1}{f_i (x^\star(t))}\nabla f_i (x^\star(t))+ A^T \hat{\nu}\]

For example, let’s consider a simple LP problem:

\[\begin{aligned} \min & \ \ c^T x\\ \text{s.t.}& \ \ Ax \leq b \end{aligned}\]

in this case, the logarithmic barrier function is given by

\[\phi(x) = - \sum_{i=1}^m \log (b_i - a_i ^T x)\]

and this LP problem can be reformulated as a problem of

\[\min_x \ \ \ t c^T x + \phi(x)\]

which implies the centrality condition:

\[tc + \sum_{i=1}^m \frac{1}{b_i - a_i ^T x^\star (t)}a_i = tc + \nabla \phi(x^\star(t)) =0\]

This implies that, at the point of $x^\star(t)$, the level curve of $\phi(x)$ is parallel to $-c$. In other words, $c^T x = c^T x^\star(t)$ is tanget to the level curve of $\phi$ through $x^\star(t)$.

Central Path Interpretations

Dual points on central path

From equation (7), rewritten below,

\[0= t\nabla f_0 (x^\star(t) ) + \sum_{i=1}^m -\frac{1}{f_i (x^\star(t))}\nabla f_i (x^\star(t))+ A^T \hat{\nu}\]

we can derive an important property of the central path: every central path yiels a dual feasible point, and hence a lower bound on the optimal value $ p^\star$. More specifically, if we define

\[\lambda_i^\star (t) = -\frac{1}{tf_i (x^\star(t))}, \ \ i=1, \cdots, m, \ \ \ \nu^\star(t) = \frac{\hat{\nu}}{t}\]

then, the equation above can be rewritten as

\[\nabla f_0 (x^\star(t)) + \sum_{i=1}^m \lambda_i ^\star(t) \nabla f_i (x^\star(t))+ A^T\nu^\star(t) =0\]

which is a 1st-order optimality condition for convex function. Thus, $x^\star(t)$ minimizes

\[L(x, \lambda^\star(t), \nu^\star(t) ) = f_0 (x) + \sum_{i=1}^m \lambda_i(t) ^\star f_i (x) + \nu^\star(t) ^T (Ax-b)\]

This implies that $(\lambda^\star(t), \nu^\star(t))$ is a dual feasible point. Note also that

\[p^\star \geq g(\lambda^\star(t), \nu^\star(t)) = L(x^\star(t), \lambda^\star(t), \nu^\star(t)) = f_0 (x^\star(t))-\frac{m}{t}\]

which provides a lower bound on the optimal value. This also confirms the intuitive idea that

\[f_0 (x^\star(t)) \rightarrow p^\star \ \ \text{as } t\rightarrow \infty\]

since

\[f_0 (x^\star(t)) - \frac{m}{t} \leq p^\star \leq f_0 (x^\star(t))\]

Interpretation via KKT conditions

We can also interpret the central path conditions as a continuous deformation of the KKT optimality conditions. A point $x$ is equal to $x^\star(t)$ iff there exists $\lambda, \nu$ such that

\[\begin{aligned} Ax&=b\\ f_i (x) & \leq 0 , \ \ \ i=1,\cdots, m\\ \lambda & \geq 0\\ \nabla f_0 (x) + \sum_{i=1}^m \lambda_i \nabla f_i (x) + A^T \nu& =0\\ -\lambda_i f_i (x) &= 1/t \end{aligned}\]

Note that the complementary slackness condition is relaxed.

Barrier Method

We have seen that

  • the point $x^\star(t)$ is $m/t$-suboptimal
  • a certificate of this accuracy is provided by the dual feasible pair $\lambda^\star(t), \nu^\star(t)$.

This suggests a very straightforward method for solving the original problem with a gauranteed specified accuracy $\epsilon$. Take $t=m/\epsilon$

\[\begin{aligned} \min_x & \ \ \frac{m}{\epsilon}f_0 (x) + \phi(x)\\ \text{s.t.}& \ \ Ax =b \end{aligned}\]

and solve it via Newton’s method.

  • obtained solution satisfies $f_0 (x) - p^\star \leq \epsilon$.
  • centering step is usually done via Newton’s method
  • large $\mu$ means fewer outer iterations, more inner Newton iterations.

References

  • S. Boyd, L.Vandenberghe, Convex Opimization, Cambridge University Press, 2004. http://stanford.edu/~boyd/cvxbook/
  • Optimization Theory and Applications, SNU EE 2021, Insoon Yang

Constrained Minimization Problems Problem Definition In this post, we will consider methods for inequality constrained minimization problem: \[ egin{aligned} \min &\ \ f_0(x)\ 	ext{s.t.}& \ \ f_i (x) \leq 0, \ i=1,\cdots, m\ & \ \ Ax=b \end{aligned}\] where $f_0, \cdots, f_m : \mathbb{R}^n ightarrow \mathbb{R}$ are convex and twice continuously differentiable with $A \in \mathbb{R}^{p	imes n}$ with $	ext{rank }A = p <n$. We assume that the problem is solvable with optimal $x^\star$ and optimal value $p^\star$. We assume that the problem is strictly feasible, which means that Slater’s constraint holds, thus strong duality holds. In other words, there exist dual optimal $\lambda^\star \in \mathbb{R}^m$ and $
u^\star \in \mathbb{R}^p $ which together with $x^\star$ satisfy the KKT conditions. \[ egin{aligned} Ax^\star & = b\ f_i (x^\star) & \leq 0 \ \ (i=1,\cdots, m)\ \lambda^\star & \geq 0\ 
abla f_0 (x^\star) +\sum_{i=1}^m \lambda^\star_i 
abla f_i (x^\star) + A^T 
u^\star & =0\ \lambda_i^\star f_i (x^\star)& =0 \ \ (i=1,\cdots, m) \end{aligned}\] Logarithmic Barrier Our goal is to approximately formulate the inequality constrained problem as an equality constraint problem to which Newton’s method can be applied. To do so, we can rewrite the problem, making the inequality constraints implicit in the objective: \[ egin{aligned} \min_x & \ \ f_0 (x) + \sum_{i=1}^m I_- (f_i (x))\ 	ext{s.t.}& \ \ Ax =b \end{aligned}\] where $I_-: \mathbb{R} ightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is the indicator function for the nonpositive reals, \[I_- (u) =  egin{cases} 0 & \ u\leq 0\ \infty & \ u>0 \end{cases}\] Now, the problem has no inequality constraints, but its objective function is not differentiable, thus Newton’s method cannot be applied. Thus, we might come up with an idea to use an approximate function of $I_-$, replaced by the function \[\hat{I}_-(u) = - rac{1}{t} \log (-u)\] which is convex and increasing in $u$, and differentiable. Substituting $\hat{I}-$ for $I-$ in above problem, we can get \[ egin{aligned} \min_x & \ \ f_0 (x) + \sum_{i=1}^m -(1/t) \log(-f_i (x))\ 	ext{s.t.}& \ \ Ax =b \end{aligned}\] If we define \[\phi(x) = - \sum_{i=1}^m \log (-f_i (x)), \ \ 	ext{dom } \phi = \{ x \ \vert \ f_i (x) <0, \ i=1, \cdots, m\}\] we can see that $\phi$ is convex and twice continuously differentiable with derivatives \[
abla \phi(x) = \sum_{i=1}^m - rac{1}{f_i (x)}
abla f_i (x)\ 
abla ^2 \phi(x) = \sum_{i=1}^m  igg [  rac{1}{f_i(x)^2 }
abla f_i (x) 
abla f_i (x)^T - rac{1}{f_i(x)}
abla ^2 f_i (x)  igg ]\] Using $\phi$, we rewrite the problem \[ egin{aligned} \min_x & \ \ tf_0 (x) + \phi(x)\ 	ext{s.t.}& \ \ Ax =b \end{aligned}\] Central Path For $t>0$, we define $x^\star(t)$ as the solution of above problem. The central path associated with this problem is defined as the set of point $x^\star(t), t>0$ which we call the central points. \[\{x^\star(t) \ \vert \ t>0\}\] Points on the central path are characterized by the following necessary and sufficient conditions: \[Ax^\star(t) =b\ f_i (x^\star(t)) <0, \ \ i=1,\cdots, m\ t
abla f_0 (x^\star(t)) + 
abla \phi(x^\star(t))+ A^T\hat{v} =0\] Note that first two conditions imply that slater’s condition, thus strong duality holds. Also, the third condition implies that there exists $\hat{
u}$ such that \[0= t
abla f_0 (x^\star(t) ) + \sum_{i=1}^m - rac{1}{f_i (x^\star(t))}
abla f_i (x^\star(t))+ A^T \hat{
u}\] For example, let’s consider a simple LP problem: \[ egin{aligned} \min & \ \ c^T x\ 	ext{s.t.}& \ \ Ax \leq b \end{aligned}\] in this case, the logarithmic barrier function is given by \[\phi(x) = - \sum_{i=1}^m \log (b_i - a_i ^T x)\] and this LP problem can be reformulated as a problem of \[\min_x \ \ \ t c^T x + \phi(x)\] which implies the centrality condition: \[tc + \sum_{i=1}^m  rac{1}{b_i - a_i ^T x^\star (t)}a_i = tc + 
abla \phi(x^\star(t)) =0\] This implies that, at the point of $x^\star(t)$, the level curve of $\phi(x)$ is parallel to $-c$. In other words, $c^T x = c^T x^\star(t)$ is tanget to the level curve of $\phi$ through $x^\star(t)$. Central Path Interpretations Dual points on central path From equation (7), rewritten below, \[0= t
abla f_0 (x^\star(t) ) + \sum_{i=1}^m - rac{1}{f_i (x^\star(t))}
abla f_i (x^\star(t))+ A^T \hat{
u}\] we can derive an important property of the central path: every central path yiels a dual feasible point, and hence a lower bound on the optimal value $ p^\star$. More specifically, if we define \[\lambda_i^\star (t) = - rac{1}{tf_i (x^\star(t))}, \ \ i=1, \cdots, m, \ \ \ 
u^\star(t) =  rac{\hat{
u}}{t}\] then, the equation above can be rewritten as \[
abla f_0 (x^\star(t)) + \sum_{i=1}^m \lambda_i ^\star(t) 
abla f_i (x^\star(t))+ A^T
u^\star(t) =0\] which is a 1st-order optimality condition for convex function. Thus, $x^\star(t)$ minimizes \[L(x, \lambda^\star(t), 
u^\star(t) ) = f_0 (x) + \sum_{i=1}^m \lambda_i(t) ^\star f_i (x) + 
u^\star(t) ^T (Ax-b)\] This implies that $(\lambda^\star(t), 
u^\star(t))$ is a dual feasible point. Note also that \[p^\star \geq g(\lambda^\star(t), 
u^\star(t)) = L(x^\star(t), \lambda^\star(t), 
u^\star(t)) = f_0 (x^\star(t))- rac{m}{t}\] which provides a lower bound on the optimal value. This also confirms the intuitive idea that \[f_0 (x^\star(t)) ightarrow p^\star \ \ 	ext{as } tightarrow \infty\] since \[f_0 (x^\star(t)) -  rac{m}{t} \leq p^\star \leq f_0 (x^\star(t))\] Interpretation via KKT conditions We can also interpret the central path conditions as a continuous deformation of the KKT optimality conditions. A point $x$ is equal to $x^\star(t)$ iff there exists $\lambda, 
u$ such that \[ egin{aligned} Ax&=b\ f_i (x) & \leq 0 , \ \ \ i=1,\cdots, m\ \lambda & \geq 0\ 
abla f_0 (x) + \sum_{i=1}^m \lambda_i 
abla f_i (x) + A^T 
u& =0\ -\lambda_i f_i (x) &= 1/t \end{aligned}\] Note that the complementary slackness condition is relaxed. Barrier Method We have seen that the point $x^\star(t)$ is $m/t$-suboptimal a certificate of this accuracy is provided by the dual feasible pair $\lambda^\star(t), 
u^\star(t)$. This suggests a very straightforward method for solving the original problem with a gauranteed specified accuracy $\epsilon$. Take $t=m/\epsilon$ \[ egin{aligned} \min_x & \ \  rac{m}{\epsilon}f_0 (x) + \phi(x)\ 	ext{s.t.}& \ \ Ax =b \end{aligned}\] and solve it via Newton’s method. obtained solution satisfies $f_0 (x) - p^\star \leq \epsilon$. centering step is usually done via Newton’s method large $\mu$ means fewer outer iterations, more inner Newton iterations. entry.title: [Optimization] Unconstrained Optimization Algorithms
entry.content:
Unconstrained Minimization Problems

Problem Definition

In this post, we will consider methods for solving the unconstrained optimization problem:

\[\min \ \ f(x)\]

where $f$ is convex and twice continuously differentiable (Hessian is well defined). We will also assume that the problem is solvable, and there exists an optimal point $x^\star$.

\[p^\star = \inf_xf(x)\]

Since $f$ is differentiable and convex, a necessary and sufficient condition for a point $x^\star$ to be optimal is:

\[\nabla f(x^\star)=0\]

Solving the unconstrained minimization problem is same as finding the solution of above equation. Usually the problem must be solved by an iterative algorithm. By this we mean an algorithm that finds the minimizing sequence $x^{(0)},x^{(1)},\cdots, x^{(k)} \in \text{dom }f$.

\[f(x^{(k)}) \rightarrow p^\star\]

The algorithm is terminated when $f(x^{(k)})-p^\star \leq \epsilon$ for predefined tolerance $\epsilon$.

Strong convexity and implications

We assume that the objective function is stronlgy convex on $S$, which means that there exists an $m>0$ such that

\[\nabla^2 f(x) \succeq mI\]

for all $x\in S$. This means that $\nabla^2 f(x) - mI$ is positive semi-definite for $\exists m$, thus $f$ has curvature greater than $m$. Strong convexity has several interesting consequences:

consequence1 For $x, y\in S$ we have

\[f(y) = f(x) + \nabla f(x)^T (y-x) + \frac{1}{2}(y-x)^T \nabla^2 f(z) (y-x)\]

for some $z$ on the line segment $[x,y]$. By the strong convexity assumption, we have

\[f(y) \geq f(x) + \nabla f(x)^T (y-x) + \frac{m}{2} \|y-x\|_2^2\]

for all $x,y \in S$.

When $m=0$, we recover the basic inequality characterizing the convexity. We can see that strong convexity ($m>0$) allows us to obtain a better lower bound on $f(y)$.

consequence2 We will then prove that above strong inequality can be used to bound $f(x) -p^\star$, which is the suboptimality of the point $x$, in terms of $|\nabla f(x) |_2$. Since the righthand side of equation (7) is a convex quadratic function of $y$, we can easily find $\tilde{y}$ that minimizes that function of $y$. \(\tilde{y} = x - (1/m) \nabla f(x)\)

Thus, we can say that

\[\begin{aligned} f(y) & \geq f(x) + \nabla f(x)^T (y-x) + \frac{m}{2} \|y-x\|_2^2\\ & \geq f(x) + \nabla f(x)^T (\tilde{y}-x) + \frac{m}{2} \|\tilde{y} - x\|_2 ^2\\ & = f(x) + \nabla f(x)^T \bigg ( -\frac{1}{m}\nabla f(x)\bigg ) + \frac{m}{2} \bigg ( \frac{1}{m} \nabla f(x) \bigg )^2\\ & = f(x) - \frac{1}{2m}\|\nabla f(x)\|_2 ^2 \end{aligned}\]

Since above inequality holds for any $y\in S$, we have

\[p^\star \geq f(x) - \frac{1}{2m} \|\nabla f(x)\|_2^2\]

This inequality shows that, if the gradient is small at a point, then the point is nearly optimal. Thus, this inequality can be interpreted as a condition for suboptimality which generalizes the optimality condition:

\[\|\nabla f(x) \|_2 \leq (2m\epsilon)^{1/2} \implies f(x)-p^\star \leq \epsilon\]

consequence3 We can also derive a bound on $|x- x^\star|_2$, the distance between $x$ and any optimal point $x^\star$, in terms of $|\nabla f(x) |_2$: \(\|x - x^\star \|_2 \leq \frac{2}{m}\|\nabla f(x) \|_2\)

to see this, we apply $y= x^\star$ to obtain

\[\begin{aligned} f(x^\star)= p^\star & \geq f(x) + \nabla f(x)^T (x^\star-x) + \frac{m}{2} \|x^\star-x\|_2^2\\ & \geq f(x) - \|\nabla f(x)\|_2 \|x^\star - x\|_2 + \frac{m}{2}\|x^\star - x\|_2 ^2 \ \ \ (\because \text{caushy-schwarz inequality}) \end{aligned}\]

since $p^\star \leq f(x)$, we can conclude that

\[- \|\nabla f(x) \|_2 \|x^\star - x\|_2 + \frac{m}{2} \|x^\star - x\|_2 ^2 \leq 0\]

which leads to the inequality in (11).

Descent Methods

Introduction

Algorithms in this section produce a minimizing sequence $x^{(k)} ,\ k= 1, \cdots, $ where

\[x^{(k+1)}:= x^{(k)}+ t^{(k)}\Delta x^{(k)} \ \ \ \text{with } f(x^{(k+1)}) < f(x^{(k)})\]

In above equation, $\Delta x$ is the search direction, $t$ is the step size.

From convexity, \(f(x+ t\Delta x) < f(x)\) implies

\[\nabla f(x)^T \Delta x <0\]

since \(f(x) > f(x+ t \Delta x) \geq f(x) + \nabla f(x)^T t \Delta x\) and $t>0$. Thus, $\Delta x$ is called a descent direction. It is typical to use $\Delta x = -\nabla f(x)$.

Line Search

In line search step, we determine the step size $t$ , which determines where along the line \(\{x + t\Delta x \ \vert \ t \in \mathbb{R}_+\}\) the next ierate will be. There are typically two types of line search:

  1. exact line search : $t$ is chosen to minimize $f$ along the ray \(\{ x+ t\Delta x \ \vert \ t \geq 0 \}\):

    \[t = \arg \min _{s \geq 0} f(x + s \Delta x)\]

    However, directly solving above minimization problem is computationally challenging.

  2. backtracking line search : there are many inexact line search method chooses the step length that can approximately minimize $f$ along the ray \(\{ x+ t\Delta x \ \vert \ t \geq 0 \}\). backtracking line search is one of them. It depends on two constants $\alpha, \beta$ with $\alpha \in (0,0.5)$ and $\beta \in (0,1)$.

    Recall that a proper descent direction $\Delta x$ will satisfy $\nabla f(x)^T \Delta x \leq 0$. Thus, backtracking line search is reducing the step size until

    \[f(x + t \Delta x) < f(x) + \alpha t \nabla f(x)^T \Delta x\]

    is satisfied.

Gradient descent method

A natural choice for the search direction $\Delta x$ is the negative direction $\Delta x = - \nabla f(x)$. The resulting algorithm is gradient descent method.

  • Stopping criterion : usually of the form

    \[\|\nabla f(x) \|_2 \leq \epsilon\]

  • convergence result : for strongly convex $f$,

    \[f(x^{(k)}) - p^\star \leq c^k [ f(x^{(0)})- p^\star],\]

    where $c \in (0,1)$ depends on $m$, $x^{(0)}$, line search type.

  • It’s main disadvantage is that its convergence rate depends on so critically on the condition number of the Hessian or sublevel sets.

Steepest descent method

The first-order Taylor approximation of $f(x+v)$ around $x$ is

\[f(x+v) \approx \hat{f}(x+v) = f(x)+ \nabla f(x) ^T v\]

where the second term on the RHS is the directional derivative of $f$ at $x$ in the direction $v$. We now raise the question of how to choose $v$ to make the directional derivative as negative as possible! To limit the effect of the length of $v$, we define a normalized steepest descent direction as

\[\Delta x_{\text{nsd}} = \arg \min \{ \nabla f(x)^T v \ \vert \ \|v \|=1 \}\]

Thus, $\Delta x_{\text{nsd}}$ is a step of unit norm that gives the largest decrease in the linear approximate of $f$.

For example, if we define a weighted quadratic norm of

\[\|x \|_P : = (x^T P x) ^{1/2}, \ \ P \in \mathbb{S}^n _{++}\]

we can determine the steepest descent direction by solving

\[\begin{aligned} \arg \min _v & \ \ \nabla f(x)^T v \\ \text{s.t.} & \ \ v^T P v = 1 \end{aligned}\]

This can is in fact finding the point $v$ on the ellipsoid that extends as far as possible along the direction $-\nabla f(x)$.

Newton’s Method

The Newton Step

For $x \in \text{dom }f$, the vector

\[\Delta x_{\text{nt}} = -\nabla^2 f(x) ^{-1} \nabla f(x)\]

is called the newton step. Positive definiteness of $\nabla^2 f(x)$ implies that

\[\nabla f(x)^T \Delta x_{\text{nt}} = - \nabla f(x)^T \nabla^2 f(x)^{-1} \nabla f(x) <0\]

unless $\nabla f(x) =0$, so the Newton step is a descent direction. The Newton step can be itnerpreted in several ways.

Interpretations

  1. $x+ \Delta x_{\text{nt}}$ minimizes second order approximation

    \[\hat{f}(x+v) = f(x) + \nabla f(x)^T v + \frac{1}{2}v^T \nabla^2 f(x) v\]

    which is a convex quadratic function of $v$. Thus, we can easily find that $v = \Delta x_{\text{nt}}$ minimizes above second-order taylor approximation.

    If $f$ is quadratic, then $x+\Delta x_{\text{nt}}$ is the exact minimizer of $f$.

  2. $\Delta x _{\text{nt}}$ is the steepest descent direction in Hessian norm

    If we define the quadratic norm defined by the Hessian $\nabla^2 f(x)$,

    \[\|u \|_{\nabla^2 f(x)} = (u^T \nabla^2 f(x) u)^{1/2}\]

    the Newton step is the steepest descent direction at $x$.

  3. $x+ \Delta x_{\text{nt}}$ solves the linearized optimality condition

    If we linearize the optimality condition $\nabla f(x^\star)=0$ near $x$, we have

    \[\nabla f(x+v) \approx \nabla f(x) + \nabla^ 2f(x)v=0\]

    which has the solution of $v= \Delta x _{\text{nt}}$. When $f: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$, this interpretation is particularly very simple. The solution of $x^\star$ of the minimization problem is characterized by $f’(x^\star)=0$, the zero-crossing of the derivative $f’$.

Newton decrement

The quantity

\[\lambda (x) := \Big [ \nabla f(x) ^T \nabla ^2 f(x)^{-1} \nabla f(x) \Big ] ^{1/2}\]

is called the Newton decrement at $x$. This is a useful quantity in Newton’s method. We can relate the Newton decrement to the quantity $f(x) - \inf_y \hat{f}(y)$ where $\hat{f}$ is the second-order approximation of $f$ at $x$. This can be used as an estimate of $f(x) - p^\star$.

\[\begin{aligned} f(x) - p^\star \approx f(x) - \inf_y \hat{f}(y) & = f(x) - \hat{f}(x+ \Delta x_{\text{nt}})\\ & = - \nabla f(x) ^T \Delta x_{\text{nt}} - \frac{1}{2}\Delta x_{\text{nt}}^T \nabla^2 f(x) \Delta x_{\text{nt}}\\ & = \frac{1}{2}\nabla f(x)^T \nabla^2 f(x)^{-1}\nabla f(x) = \frac{1}{2} \lambda(x)^2 \end{aligned}\]

Also, this newton decrement $\lambda(x)$ is equal to the norm of the Newton step in the quadratic Hessian norm.

\[\lambda(x) = \Big [ \Delta x_{\text{nt}}^T \nabla^2 f(x) \Delta x_{\text{nt}} \Big ]^{1/2}\]

Finally, recall that backtracking line search procedure requires a step direction where $\nabla f(x)^T \Delta x <0$. Newton decrement again appears when calculating this

\[\nabla f(x)^T \Delta_{\text{nt}} = -\lambda(x)^2\]

Newton’s Method

Newton’s Method: Convergence Analysis

Assumption

We assume that $f$ is twice continuously differentiable, and strongly convex with constant $m$ for all $x$.

\[\nabla^2 f(x) \succeq mI\]

In addition, we assume that the Hessian of $f$ is Lipschitz continuous on $S$ with constant $L$.

\[\|\nabla^2 f(x) - \nabla^2 f(y) \|_2 \leq L \|x- y \|_2\]

for all $x, y \in S$. The coefficient $L$ measrues how well $f$ can be approximated by a quadratic model. Intuition suggests that Newton’s method will work very well for a function whose quadratic model varies slowly (small $L$).

Outline of the proof

We will show that there are number $\eta$ and $\gamma$ with $0<\eta \leq m^2 /L$ and $\gamma >0$ such that the following hold.

  • (Damped newton phase) If \(\| \nabla f(x^{(k)})\|_2 \geq \eta\), then \(f(x^{(k+1)})-f(x^{(k)}) \leq -\gamma\)

    • most iterations require backtracking steps
    • function value decreases by at least $\gamma$
    • if $p^\star >-\infty$, this phase ends after at most \(f(x^{(0)}- p^\star) / \gamma\) iterations.

  • (Quadratic convergent phase) If \(\|\nabla f(x^{(k)})\|_2 < \eta\), then the backtracking line search selects \(t^{(k)}=1\) and

    \[\frac{L}{2m^2} \|\nabla f(x^{(k+1)})\|_2 \leq \bigg ( \frac{L}{2m^2} \| \nabla f(x^{(k)})\|_2 \bigg )^2\]
    • all iterations use step size $t=1$.
    • $|\nabla f(x)|_2$ converges to zero quadratically.

References

  • S. Boyd, L.Vandenberghe, Convex Opimization, Cambridge University Press, 2004. http://stanford.edu/~boyd/cvxbook/
  • Optimization Theory and Applications, SNU EE 2021, Insoon Yang

abla f(x)\) Thus, we can say that \[ egin{aligned} f(y) & \geq f(x) + 
abla f(x)^T (y-x) +  rac{m}{2} \|y-x\|_2^2\ & \geq f(x) + 
abla f(x)^T (	ilde{y}-x) +  rac{m}{2} \|	ilde{y} - x\|_2 ^2\ & = f(x) + 
abla f(x)^T  igg ( - rac{1}{m}
abla f(x) igg ) +  rac{m}{2}  igg (  rac{1}{m} 
abla f(x)  igg )^2\ & = f(x) -  rac{1}{2m}\|
abla f(x)\|_2 ^2 \end{aligned}\] Since above inequality holds for any $y\in S$, we have \[p^\star \geq f(x) -  rac{1}{2m} \|
abla f(x)\|_2^2\] This inequality shows that, if the gradient is small at a point, then the point is nearly optimal. Thus, this inequality can be interpreted as a condition for suboptimality which generalizes the optimality condition: \[\|
abla f(x) \|_2 \leq (2m\epsilon)^{1/2} \implies f(x)-p^\star \leq \epsilon\] consequence3 We can also derive a bound on $|x- x^\star|_2$, the distance between $x$ and any optimal point $x^\star$, in terms of $|
abla f(x) |_2$: \(\|x - x^\star \|_2 \leq  rac{2}{m}\|
abla f(x) \|_2\) to see this, we apply $y= x^\star$ to obtain \[ egin{aligned} f(x^\star)= p^\star & \geq f(x) + 
abla f(x)^T (x^\star-x) +  rac{m}{2} \|x^\star-x\|_2^2\ & \geq f(x) - \|
abla f(x)\|_2 \|x^\star - x\|_2 +  rac{m}{2}\|x^\star - x\|_2 ^2 \ \ \ ( ecause 	ext{caushy-schwarz inequality}) \end{aligned}\] since $p^\star \leq f(x)$, we can conclude that \[- \|
abla f(x) \|_2 \|x^\star - x\|_2 +  rac{m}{2} \|x^\star - x\|_2 ^2 \leq 0\] which leads to the inequality in (11). Descent Methods Introduction Algorithms in this section produce a minimizing sequence $x^{(k)} ,\ k= 1, \cdots, $ where \[x^{(k+1)}:= x^{(k)}+ t^{(k)}\Delta x^{(k)} \ \ \ 	ext{with } f(x^{(k+1)}) < f(x^{(k)})\] In above equation, $\Delta x$ is the search direction, $t$ is the step size. From convexity, \(f(x+ t\Delta x) < f(x)\) implies \[
abla f(x)^T \Delta x <0\] since \(f(x) > f(x+ t \Delta x) \geq f(x) + 
abla f(x)^T t \Delta x\) and $t>0$. Thus, $\Delta x$ is called a descent direction. It is typical to use $\Delta x = -
abla f(x)$. Line Search In line search step, we determine the step size $t$ , which determines where along the line \(\{x + t\Delta x \ \vert \ t \in \mathbb{R}_+\}\) the next ierate will be. There are typically two types of line search: exact line search : $t$ is chosen to minimize $f$ along the ray \(\{ x+ t\Delta x \ \vert \ t \geq 0 \}\): \[t = \arg \min _{s \geq 0} f(x + s \Delta x)\] However, directly solving above minimization problem is computationally challenging. backtracking line search : there are many inexact line search method chooses the step length that can approximately minimize $f$ along the ray \(\{ x+ t\Delta x \ \vert \ t \geq 0 \}\). backtracking line search is one of them. It depends on two constants $\alpha,  eta$ with $\alpha \in (0,0.5)$ and $ eta \in (0,1)$. Recall that a proper descent direction $\Delta x$ will satisfy $
abla f(x)^T \Delta x \leq 0$. Thus, backtracking line search is reducing the step size until \[f(x + t \Delta x) < f(x) + \alpha t 
abla f(x)^T \Delta x\] is satisfied. Gradient descent method A natural choice for the search direction $\Delta x$ is the negative direction $\Delta x = - 
abla f(x)$. The resulting algorithm is gradient descent method. Stopping criterion : usually of the form \[\|
abla f(x) \|_2 \leq \epsilon\] convergence result : for strongly convex $f$, \[f(x^{(k)}) - p^\star \leq c^k [ f(x^{(0)})- p^\star],\] where $c \in (0,1)$ depends on $m$, $x^{(0)}$, line search type. It’s main disadvantage is that its convergence rate depends on so critically on the condition number of the Hessian or sublevel sets. Steepest descent method The first-order Taylor approximation of $f(x+v)$ around $x$ is \[f(x+v) \approx \hat{f}(x+v) = f(x)+ 
abla f(x) ^T v\] where the second term on the RHS is the directional derivative of $f$ at $x$ in the direction $v$. We now raise the question of how to choose $v$ to make the directional derivative as negative as possible! To limit the effect of the length of $v$, we define a normalized steepest descent direction as \[\Delta x_{	ext{nsd}} = \arg \min \{ 
abla f(x)^T v \ \vert \ \|v \|=1 \}\] Thus, $\Delta x_{	ext{nsd}}$ is a step of unit norm that gives the largest decrease in the linear approximate of $f$. For example, if we define a weighted quadratic norm of \[\|x \|_P : = (x^T P x) ^{1/2}, \ \ P \in \mathbb{S}^n _{++}\] we can determine the steepest descent direction by solving \[ egin{aligned} \arg \min _v & \ \ 
abla f(x)^T v \ 	ext{s.t.} & \ \ v^T P v = 1 \end{aligned}\] This can is in fact finding the point $v$ on the ellipsoid that extends as far as possible along the direction $-
abla f(x)$. Newton’s Method The Newton Step For $x \in 	ext{dom }f$, the vector \[\Delta x_{	ext{nt}} = -
abla^2 f(x) ^{-1} 
abla f(x)\] is called the newton step. Positive definiteness of $
abla^2 f(x)$ implies that \[
abla f(x)^T \Delta x_{	ext{nt}} = - 
abla f(x)^T 
abla^2 f(x)^{-1} 
abla f(x) <0\] unless $
abla f(x) =0$, so the Newton step is a descent direction. The Newton step can be itnerpreted in several ways. Interpretations $x+ \Delta x_{	ext{nt}}$ minimizes second order approximation \[\hat{f}(x+v) = f(x) + 
abla f(x)^T v +  rac{1}{2}v^T 
abla^2 f(x) v\] which is a convex quadratic function of $v$. Thus, we can easily find that $v = \Delta x_{	ext{nt}}$ minimizes above second-order taylor approximation. If $f$ is quadratic, then $x+\Delta x_{	ext{nt}}$ is the exact minimizer of $f$. $\Delta x _{	ext{nt}}$ is the steepest descent direction in Hessian norm If we define the quadratic norm defined by the Hessian $
abla^2 f(x)$, \[\|u \|_{
abla^2 f(x)} = (u^T 
abla^2 f(x) u)^{1/2}\] the Newton step is the steepest descent direction at $x$. $x+ \Delta x_{	ext{nt}}$ solves the linearized optimality condition If we linearize the optimality condition $
abla f(x^\star)=0$ near $x$, we have \[
abla f(x+v) \approx 
abla f(x) + 
abla^ 2f(x)v=0\] which has the solution of $v= \Delta x _{	ext{nt}}$. When $f: \mathbb{R} ightarrow \mathbb{R}$, this interpretation is particularly very simple. The solution of $x^\star$ of the minimization problem is characterized by $f’(x^\star)=0$, the zero-crossing of the derivative $f’$. Newton decrement The quantity \[\lambda (x) := \Big [ 
abla f(x) ^T 
abla ^2 f(x)^{-1} 
abla f(x) \Big ] ^{1/2}\] is called the Newton decrement at $x$. This is a useful quantity in Newton’s method. We can relate the Newton decrement to the quantity $f(x) - \inf_y \hat{f}(y)$ where $\hat{f}$ is the second-order approximation of $f$ at $x$. This can be used as an estimate of $f(x) - p^\star$. \[ egin{aligned} f(x) - p^\star \approx f(x) - \inf_y \hat{f}(y) & = f(x) - \hat{f}(x+ \Delta x_{	ext{nt}})\ & = - 
abla f(x) ^T \Delta x_{	ext{nt}} -  rac{1}{2}\Delta x_{	ext{nt}}^T 
abla^2 f(x) \Delta x_{	ext{nt}}\ & =  rac{1}{2}
abla f(x)^T 
abla^2 f(x)^{-1}
abla f(x) =  rac{1}{2} \lambda(x)^2 \end{aligned}\] Also, this newton decrement $\lambda(x)$ is equal to the norm of the Newton step in the quadratic Hessian norm. \[\lambda(x) = \Big [ \Delta x_{	ext{nt}}^T 
abla^2 f(x) \Delta x_{	ext{nt}} \Big ]^{1/2}\] Finally, recall that backtracking line search procedure requires a step direction where $
abla f(x)^T \Delta x <0$. Newton decrement again appears when calculating this \[
abla f(x)^T \Delta_{	ext{nt}} = -\lambda(x)^2\] Newton’s Method Newton’s Method: Convergence Analysis Assumption We assume that $f$ is twice continuously differentiable, and strongly convex with constant $m$ for all $x$. \[
abla^2 f(x) \succeq mI\] In addition, we assume that the Hessian of $f$ is Lipschitz continuous on $S$ with constant $L$. \[\|
abla^2 f(x) - 
abla^2 f(y) \|_2 \leq L \|x- y \|_2\] for all $x, y \in S$. The coefficient $L$ measrues how well $f$ can be approximated by a quadratic model. Intuition suggests that Newton’s method will work very well for a function whose quadratic model varies slowly (small $L$). Outline of the proof We will show that there are number $\eta$ and $\gamma$ with $0<\eta \leq m^2 /L$ and $\gamma >0$ such that the following hold. (Damped newton phase) If \(\| 
abla f(x^{(k)})\|_2 \geq \eta\), then \(f(x^{(k+1)})-f(x^{(k)}) \leq -\gamma\) most iterations require backtracking steps function value decreases by at least $\gamma$ if $p^\star >-\infty$, this phase ends after at most \(f(x^{(0)}- p^\star) / \gamma\) iterations. (Quadratic convergent phase) If \(\|
abla f(x^{(k)})\|_2 < \eta\), then the backtracking line search selects \(t^{(k)}=1\) and \[ rac{L}{2m^2} \|
abla f(x^{(k+1)})\|_2 \leq  igg (  rac{L}{2m^2} \| 
abla f(x^{(k)})\|_2  igg )^2\] all iterations use step size $t=1$. $|
abla f(x)|_2$ converges to zero quadratically. References S. Boyd, L.Vandenberghe, Convex Opimization, Cambridge University Press, 2004. http://stanford.edu/~boyd/cvxbook/ Optimization Theory and Applications, SNU EE 2021, Insoon Yang

entry.title: [Optimization] Statistical Learning
entry.content:
Maximum Likelihood (ML) estimation

Introduction

Consider a family of probability distributions on $\mathbb{R}^m$, parameterized by a vector $\mathbf{x}\in \mathbb{R}^n$ with densities $p_\mathbf{x}(\cdot)$. When considered as a function of $\mathbf{x}$, for fixed $\mathbf{y}\in \mathbb{R}^m$, the function $p_{\mathbf{x}}(\mathbf{y})$ is called the likelihood function. It is more convenient to work with its lograithm, thus we define a log-likelihood function, denoted by $l$:

\[l(x) = \log p_{\mathbf{x}}(\mathbf{y})\]

One can add constraints on the values of the parameter $\mathbf{x}$, represening the prior knowledge about $\mathbf{x}$. These constraints can be explicilty given, or incorporated into the likelihood function by assigning $p_{\mathbf{x}}(\mathbf{y})=0$ for all $\mathbf{y}$ if $\mathbf{x} \not \in C$.

Now, consider the problem of estimating the value of the parameter $\mathbf{x}$, based on observing one sample $\mathbf{y}$ from the distribution. ML estimation is to estimate $\mathbf{x}$ as

\[\mathbf{x}_{ml} = \arg\max_{\mathbf{x}}p_{\mathbf{x}}({\mathbf{y}})\]

which is a convex optimization problem, if the log-likelihood function $l$ is concave for each value $\mathbf{y}$, and the feasible set $C$ can be described by a set of linear equality and conve inequality constraints.

Linear measurements with IID noise

Consider a linear measurement model:

\(y_ i = a_i ^T x + v_i , \ i=1, \cdots, m\) where $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is a vector of parametes to be estimated, $y_i \in \mathbb{R}$ are the measured / observed quantities, and $v_i \in \mathbb{R}$ are the measruement erros or noise. Assuming that $v_i$ are independent, identically distributed (IID), with density $p$ on $\mathbb{R}$. Then, the likelihood function is:

\[p_x(y) = \prod _{i=1}^m p(y_i - a_i ^T x)\]

so the log-likelihood function is

\[l(x) = \log p_x(y) = \sum_{i=1}^m \log p(y_i - a_i ^T x)\]

thus the ML estimate is any optimal point for the problem

\[\max_x \sum_{i=1}^m \log p(y_i - a_i^T x)\]

  1. If $v$ is gaussian noise

    Knowing that guaussian noise $\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$:

    \[p(z) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \sigma^2}}e^{-\frac{z^2}{2\sigma^2}}\]

    ML estimate is an optimal solution to

    \[\max_x \sum_{i=1}^m \log p(y_i - a_i^T x) \\ \iff \max_x \sum_{i=1}^m -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}(y_i -a_i ^T x)^2\\ \iff \min_x \sum_{i=1}^m \frac{1}{2\sigma^2} (a_i ^T x - y_i )^2\]

    which is a least squares solution.

  2. If $v$ is Laplacian noise

    Knowing that laplacian noise is defined by

    \[p(z) = \frac{1}{2a} e^{-\frac{|z|}{a}}\]

    ML estimate is an optimal solution to

    \[\max_x \sum_{i=1}^m \log p(y_i - a_i^T x) \\ \iff \max_x \sum_{i=1}^m -\frac{1}{a}|y_i - a_i^T x |\\ \iff \min_x \sum_{i=1}^m \frac{1}{a}|y_i - a_i^T x |\]

    which is an $l_1$-norm solution.

  3. If $v$ is Uniform noise

    Uniform noise on $[-a,a]$:

    \[p(z) = \frac{1}{2a}, \ \ \forall z \in [-a,a]\]

    Thus, we have

    \[l(x)= \begin{cases} -m \log(2a) & \text{if } |a_i^T x - y_i | \leq a\\ -\infty & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}\]

    ML estimate is any $x$ with \(\vert a_i^T x - y_i \vert \leq a\) for all $i$.

Logistic Regression

Consider a random variable \(y \in \{0,1\}\) with

\[\text{prob} (y=1) = p, \ \ \text{prob}(y=0)= 1-p\]

We want to estimate the probability of being $y=1$ for a given observed state $u$. To do so, we are going to estimate the parameters $a, b$ from $m$ observations $(u_i, y_i)$, while the proability $p$ is parametrized by:

\[p = \frac{\exp{(a^Tu +b)}}{1+\exp{(a^T u+b)}} = \sigma(a^Tu+b)\]

where $\sigma(z)$ is a sigmoid function

\[\sigma(z) = \frac{e^z}{1+e^z}\]

Assuming $y_1, \cdots, y_k = 1$ and $y_{k+1}, \cdots, y_n =0$, the likelihood function has the form

\[p_{a,b}(y) = \prod _{i=1}^k p_i \prod_{i=k+1}^n (1-p_i)\]

Thus, the log-likelihood function is given by

\[\begin{aligned} l(a,b) & = \sum_{i=1}^k \log p_i + \sum_{i=k+1}^n \log (1-p_i)\\ & = \sum_{i=1}^k (a^T u _i +b) - \sum_{i=1}^n \log (1+ \exp(a^T u_i + b) ) \end{aligned}\]

which is a concave maximization problem in $a,b$.

Maximum a posteriori probability (MAP) estimation

Introduction

Maximum a posteriori probability (MAP) estimation can be considered as a Bayesian version of ML estimation, with a prior probability density on the underlying parameter $x$. In MAP, we assume that $x$ and $y$ are both random variables with a joint probability density $p(x,y)$.

The prior density of $x$ is given by

\[p_x (x) = \int p (x,y) dy\]

This density represents our prior information about what the values of the vector $x$ might be, before we observe the vector $y$. Similariy, the prior density of $y$ is given by

\[p_y (y) = \int p(x,y) dx\]

This density represents the prior information about what the measurement or observation vector $y$ will be.

The conditional density of $y$, given $x$, is given by

\[p_{y|x} (x,y) = \frac{p(x,y)}{p_x (x)}\]

In the MAP estimation method, \(p_{y \vert x}\) plays the role of the parameter dependent density $p_x$ in the ML estimation setup. The conditional density of $x$, given $y$, is given by

\[p_{x|y} (x,y) = \frac{p(x,y)}{p_y (y)} = p_{y|x}(x,y) \frac{p_x (x)}{p_y(y)}\]

When we substitute the observed value $y$ into $p_{x \vert y}$, we obtain the posterior density of $x$. It represents our knowledge of $x$ after observation.

Thus, in MAP estimation method, our estimate of $x$, given the observation $y$, is given by

\[\begin{aligned} \hat{x}_{map} &= \arg \max_x p_{x|y} (x,y)\\ & = \arg \max_x p_{y|x}(x,y)p_x(x)/p_y (y)\\ & = \arg \max_x p_{y|x} (x,y) p_x (x) \\ & = \arg \max_x p(x,y) \end{aligned}\]

We are taking an estimate of $x$ as the value that maximizes the conditional density of $x$, given the observed value of $y$. The only difference between the ML estimate is that, we are taking the $p_x(x)$ term into account, which represents our prior knowledge of $x$. If this $p_x(x)$ is constant, MAP estimation is identical to ML estimation.

Taking logarithms, we can express the MAP estimate as

\[\hat{x}_{map} \in \arg \max _x \bigg [ \log p_{y|x} (x,y) + \log p_x (x) \bigg ]\]

This problem is convex if the log-likelihood function is concave, and the prior density of $x$ is log-concave.

Linear measurements with IID noise

Suppose that $x\in \mathbb{R}^n$ and $y \in \mathbb{R}^m$ are related by

\[y_i = a_i ^T x + v_i, \ \ i=1, \cdots, m\]

where $v_i$ are IID with density $p_v$ on $\mathbb{R}$, and $x$ has prior density $p_x$ on $\mathbb{R}^n$. The joint density of $x$ and $y$ is then

\[p(x,y) =p_x (x) \prod _{i=1}^m p_v (y_i - a_i^T x)\]

and the MAP estimate can be found by solving the optimization problem

\[\max _x \log p_x (x) + \sum_{i=1}^m \log p_v (y_i - a_i^T x)\]

For example, if $v_i$ are uniform on $[-a,a]$, and the prior of $x$ is Guassian with mean $\bar{x}$ and covariance $\Sigma$, the MAP estimation is found by solving the QP

\[\begin{aligned} \max_x & \ \ (x-\bar{x})^T \Sigma^{-1} (x-\bar{x})\\ \text{s.t.} & \ \ \|Ax-y \|_\infty \leq a \end{aligned}\]

Nonparametric distribution estimation

Let $X$ be the random variable with values in \(\{\alpha_1, \cdots, \alpha_n \} \subset \mathbb{R}\). Then the distribution of $X$ can be characterized by $p\in \mathbb{R}^n$ with $\text{prob }(X= \alpha_k)=p_k$. Clearly, $p$ satisfies $p\geq 0, \ \mathbf{1}^Tp = 1$.

Conversely, if $p\in \mathbb{R}^n$ satisfies $p\geq 0, \ \mathbf{1}^T p =1$, then it defines a probability distribution for a random variable $X$, defined as $\text{prob } (X=\alpha_k) = p_k$. Thus, we can define a probability simplex

\[\{ p \in \mathbb{R}^n \ \vert \ p \geq 0 , \ \mathbf{1}^T p =1 \}\]

which has a one-to-one correspondence with all possible probability distributions for $X$.

Our question in this section is:

How should we estimate the distribution $p$ based on a combination of prior information, and possibly, observations and measurements. Keep in mind, we don’t want a parameterized probability (density) function, but a probability vector.

Prior Information

How can we express the prior information about $p$? It can be expressed in terms of linear equality constraints or inequalities. If $f:\mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is any function, then

\[\mathbb{E} f(X) = \sum_{i=1}^n p_i f(\alpha_i)\]

is a linear function of $p$. Also, if we consider the $\text{prob } (X \in C)$ for $C\subseteq \mathbb{R}$, this can also be expressed as a linear function of $p$.

\[\text{prob } (X \in C) = c^T p , \ \ \ c_i = \begin{cases} 1 & \ \alpha_i \in C\\ 0 & \ \alpha_i \not \in C \end{cases}\]

These examples show that, known expected values of certain functions or known probabiliteis of certain sets can be incorporated as linear equality constraints on $p\in \mathbb{R}^n$. Let’s look at some more examples:

  1. suppose we know that $X$ has mean $\mathbb{E}X = \alpha$, and second moment $\mathbb{E}X^2 = \beta$, and $\text{prob } (X\geq 0) \leq 0.3$. This can be expressed as a constraint:

    \[\mathbb{E}X = \sum_{i=1}^n \alpha_i p_i = \alpha, \ \ \mathbb{E}X^2 = \sum_{i=1}^n \alpha_i ^2 p_i = \beta, \ \ \sum_{\alpha_i \geq 0} p_i \leq 0.3\]

  2. variance of $X$ given as a constraint:

    \[\text{var } X = \mathbb{E}X^2 - (\mathbb{E}X)^2 = \sum_{i=1}^n \alpha_i ^2 p_i - \bigg ( \sum_{i=1}^n \alpha_i p_i \bigg )^2\]

    In this case, the first term is a linear function of $p$, and the second term is concave quadratic in $p$. Thus, the constraint of

    \[\text{var}(X) \geq \sigma^2\]

    is a convex constraint, while the \(\text{var}(X) \leq \sigma^2\) is not a conevx constraint.

  3. Suppose $A, B \subseteq \mathbb{R}$. COnsider a conditional probability of $A$ given $B$.

    \[\text{prob} (X \in A \vert X\in B) = \frac{\text{prob}(X\in A\cap B)}{\text{prob}(X\in B)}\]

    This function is linear-fractional in $p\in \mathbb{R}^n$, since we can express $\text{prob}(X\in C)$ for any $C$ as a linear function of $p$.

    Thus, if we want to impose a prior constraint of

    \[l \leq \text{prob}(X \in A | X \in B) = \frac{c^T p}{d^T p} \leq u\]

    as the linear inequality constraints on $p$

    \[l d^T \leq c^T p \leq u d^T\]

  4. One might impose a entropy constraint:

    \[-\sum_{i=1}^n p_i \log p_i\]

    which is a concave function of $p$. Be careful with the inequality direction to make it as a convex constraint!

  5. Kullback-Leibeler divergence of another distribution $q$ from the distribution $p$:

    \[D_{KL}(p \| q) = \sum_{i=1}^n p_i \log (p_i / q_i )\]

Bounding probabilities and expected values

Using above techniques, we might easily convert the prior information as a form of feasible set $\mathcal{P}$. Using this, we can then compute upper / lower bounds on the expected value of a function, or a probability of a set. For example, to determine a lower bound on $\mathbb{E} f(X)$ over all distributions that satisfy the prior information $\mathcal{P}$, we should solve the convex problem:

\[\begin{aligned} \min_p & \ \ \sum_{i=1}^n f(\alpha_i ) p_i\\ \text{s.t.} & \ \ p \in \mathcal{P} \end{aligned}\]

Maximum Likelihood Estimation

We can use maximum likelihood estimation to estimate $p$ based on observations from the distribution. Suppose we observe $N$ independent samples $x_1, \cdots, x_N$ from the distribution . Let $k_i$ denote the number of these samples with value $\alpha_i$, so that $k_1 + \cdots+ k_n = N $. The log-likelihood of the function is then

\[l(p) = \sum_{i=1}^n k_i \log p_i\]

Thus, the ML estimate of $p$ can be found by solving the convex optimiation problem of

\[\begin{aligned} \max_p & \ \ l(p) = \sum_{i=1}^n k_i \log p_i\\ \text{s.t.}& \ \ p \in \mathcal{P} \end{aligned}\]

Maximum Entropy

The maximum entropy distribution consistent with the prior assumptions can be found by solving the convex problem

\[\begin{aligned} \max_p & \ \ \sum_{i=1}^n p_i \log p_i\\ \text{s.t.}& \ \ p \in \mathcal{P} \end{aligned}\]

This is the most equivocal, most random among those consistent with the prior information.

Minimum KL Divergence

Find distribution $p$ that has minimum KL divergence from a given distribution $q$.

\[\begin{aligned} \max_p & \ \ \sum_{i=1}^n p_i \log (p_i/q_i)\\ \text{s.t.}& \ \ p \in \mathcal{P} \end{aligned}\]

References

  • S. Boyd, L.Vandenberghe, Convex Opimization, Cambridge University Press, 2004. http://stanford.edu/~boyd/cvxbook/
  • Optimization Theory and Applications, SNU EE 2021, Insoon Yang
